items.foco: Triad: postprandial pain, weight loss, abdominal murmur
items.title: Classic triad of chronic intestinal ischemia
items.contentHtml:
Classic triad

  • Postprandial pain (intestinal angina) + involuntary weight loss + abdominal murmur in the epigastrium constitute the classic triad, although it is only present in ~30% of cases.
  • Postprandial pain appears 20-30 minutes after ingestion and lasts 1-3 hours; the patient develops sitophobia (fear of eating) due to the pain, which explains the weight loss.
  • The abdominal murmur is systolic, audible in the epigastrium or mesogastrium, suggesting significant stenosis (>70%) in the celiac trunk or superior mesenteric trunk.
  • DDx: peptic ulcer, chronic pancreatitis, gastric neoplasia; The lack of response to PPI indicates ischemia.

items.foco: Intestinal angina: epigastric pain 30 minutes after eating
items.title: Intestinal angina: key temporal presentation
items.contentHtml:
Intestinal angina

  • Intestinal angina is colicky epigastric or mesogastric pain that appears 20-30 minutes after eating, due to postprandial metabolic demand that exceeds the stenosed arterial flow.
  • The pain lasts 1-3 hours and can radiate to the back; It subsides with rest and fasting, similar to coronary angina.
  • Askable fact: unlike peptic ulcer, the pain does not improve with antacids and is exacerbated with heavy meals.
  • The progression of pain (more frequent with smaller meals) indicates worsening of the stenosis.

items.title: Mesenteric atherosclerosis as the main etiology
items.contentHtml:
Mesenteric atherosclerosis

  • Mesenteric atherosclerosis is the most common cause of chronic intestinal ischemia (>90% of cases), typically affecting the ostium of the visceral arteries.
  • Atherosclerotic plaques are usually located in the first 2 cm of the celiac trunk and superior mesenteric; the inferior mesenteric is affected less frequently.
  • Risk factors: smoking, DM, HBP, dyslipidemia, age >60 years.
  • Cheating: other rare causes include fibromuscular dysplasia, vasculitis (Takayasu, polyarteritis nodosa) and median arcuate ligament syndrome.

items.foco: Affected arteries: superior and inferior mesenteric and celiac arteries
items.title: Arteries involved in chronic ischemia
items.contentHtml:
Affected arteries

  • The three main arteries are celiac trunk, superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and inferior mesenteric artery (IMA).
  • The SMA is the most clinically relevant because it supplies the jejunum, ileum and right colon; Its isolated stenosis rarely causes symptoms due to pancreaticoduodenal collaterals.
  • For symptomatic chronic intestinal ischemia to appear, significant stenosis (>70%) is required in at least two of the three arteries, due to the rich collateral circulation.
  • The celiac trunk supplies the stomach, proximal duodenum and liver; The AMI irrigates the left colon and upper rectum.

items.foco: Superior mesenteric artery syndrome: compression by ligament of Treitz
items.title: Superior mesenteric artery syndrome
items.contentHtml:
SMA (Wilkie) syndrome

  • Superior mesenteric artery syndrome (Wilkie syndrome) is a rare cause of duodenal obstruction due to extrinsic compression of the third portion of the duodenum between the SMA and the aorta, not due to atherosclerosis.
  • Predisposing factors: rapid weight loss (reduction of the angle aortomesenteric <25°), scoliosis, previous abdominal surgery.
  • Clinic: postprandial epigastric pain, bilious vomiting, sensation of fullness, weight loss. DDx: is differentiated from atherosclerotic ischemia by the absence of an abdominal murmur and by CT angiography findings (angle <22° and aortomesenteric distance <8 mm).
  • Initial treatment: weight gain, fractional meals; if it fails, laparoscopic duodenojejunostomy.

items.foco: Diagnosis: CT angiography with contrast (gold standard)
items.title: CT angiography as a diagnostic gold standard
items.contentHtml:
CT angiography

  • Contrast-enhanced CT angiography (computed tomography angiography) is the gold standard for the diagnosis of chronic intestinal ischemia, with sensitivity >95% and specificity >98%.
  • Key finding: stenosis >70% of the luminal diameter in at least two visceral arteries, with visualization of plaques calcified or non-calcified.
  • Allows collateral evaluation (Riolan's arch, Drummond artery) and to rule out other causes of abdominal pain (neoplasms, pancreatitis).
  • Alternatives: Doppler ultrasound (peak systolic flow >275 cm/s in SMA suggests stenosis >70%), but it is operator-dependent; Conventional angiography is reserved for doubtful cases or simultaneous intervention.

items.foco: Significant stenosis: >70% in at least two arteries
items.title: Significant stenosis criterion
items.contentHtml:
Significant stenosis

  • Significant stenosis is defined as a reduction in luminal diameter >70% (or cross-sectional area >80%) in at least two of the three visceral arteries.
  • Isolated stenosis of an artery rarely causes symptoms due to collateral circulation (pancreaticoduodenal arches, Riolan arches).
  • Functional threshold: blood pressure distal to the stenosis must fall <40 mmHg for ischemia to appear during postprandial demand.
  • In practice, a stenosis >50% in SMA + >70% in the celiac trunk is already considered hemodynamically relevant if there are symptoms.

items.title: Revascularization as definitive treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment

  • Revascularization is indicated in symptomatic patients with significant stenosis; The current first line is the endovascular stent (angioplasty with stent) due to less morbidity and hospital stay.
  • The stent is preferred in ostial lesions of the celiac trunk and AMS; the 2-year restenosis rate is ~20-30%.
  • Surgical revascularization (aortomesenteric bypass or reimplantation) is reserved for endovascular failure, highly calcified lesions or young patients with long life expectancy.
  • Adjunctive medical management: antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 100 mg/day + clopidogrel 75 mg/day for 1 month post-stent), control of risk factors and fractional diet.

items.foco: Difference with acute ischemia: absence of initial peritonitis
items.title: Key difference with acute mesenteric ischemia
items.contentHtml:
Chronic ischemia vs. acute

  • In chronic intestinal ischemia there are no signs of peritonitis at the beginning, unlike acute mesenteric ischemia which progresses rapidly to infarction and peritonitis.
  • chronic ischemia has an insidious evolution (months to years) with recurrent postprandial pain and weight loss; the abdomen is usually soft and not painful on palpation.
  • In acute ischemia (embolus or thrombosis) the pain is severe, disproportionate to the physical examination, and peritonitis appears when there is transmural necrosis (6-12 hours).
  • Trap: chronic ischemia can decompensate to acute thrombosis if the plaque fissures or cardiac output falls (AMI, arrhythmias).

items.foco: Risk factors: smoking, DM, HTN, dyslipidemia
items.title: Cardiovascular risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors

  • The same as for systemic atherosclerotic disease: smoking, diabetes mellitus, hypertension and dyslipidemia (especially elevated LDL).
  • Smoking is the most strongly associated factor (OR ~3.5) and accelerates the progression of the disease. stenosis.
  • DM contributes to endothelial dysfunction and intimal calcification; HTN increases wall stress.
  • Others: age >60 years, male sex, concomitant peripheral and coronary artery disease (present in 40-60% of patients).

items.foco: Complication: intestinal infarction if it progresses to thrombosis
items.title: Progression to intestinal infarction
items.contentHtml:
Acute complication

  • The most feared complication is intestinal infarction (transmural necrosis) due to acute thrombosis of a previously stenotic artery, with mortality >50% if intervention is not performed within 6-8 hours.
  • Precipitating factors: systemic hypoperfusion (shock, AMI, hemorrhage), prothrombotic state, rupture of plaque.
  • Clinic: sudden intense abdominal pain, distention, vomiting, hematochezia, lactic acidosis (>2 mmol/L), peritonitis.
  • Management: emergency laparotomy with intestinal resection + embolectomy/bypass; The prognosis depends on the time of ischemia and the extent of viable intestine.

items.foco: Suspect in elderly people with chronic abdominal pain and weight loss
items.title: Clinical suspicion profile
items.contentHtml:
Clinical suspicion

  • Chronic intestinal ischemia should be suspected in elderly patients (>60 years) with chronic recurrent abdominal pain, involuntary weight loss and cardiovascular risk factors.
  • Weight loss (>5% in 6 months) is a key fact that differentiates it from other causes of abdominal pain such as bowel syndrome irritable or functional dyspepsia.
  • In an elderly person with postprandial pain, sitophobia and abdominal murmur, angioCT should be requested early to avoid diagnostic delays.
  • DDx: gastrointestinal neoplasia (rule out with endoscopy + CT), chronic pancreatitis (elevated amylase/lipase, pancreatic calcifications), peptic ulcer (response to IBP).
